it's so funny how this isn't even THAT much of an "original idea", it's a story as old as time, just painted in fresh new paint and that's all it really takes most of the time, because we live in a world where actual, real and genuine creativity, like creating something completely new is almost impossible, so the obvious solution is to take old concepts, some tried and true formulas, and just play around with them a little bit, you'd be surprised how refreshing such a simple thing could be.

author: @Ky_988
text: KPDH is girl power done right. Yes, there’s competition between men and women (Huntrix vs Saja Boys), but it doesn’t degrade men or inflate toxic egos at the expense of good storytelling. Instead, it treats both genders with respect, portraying them as real people. Not walking agendas. The characters are allowed to be themselves: women as women, men as men. They can fall in love, make mistakes, and sacrifice for one another. It feels natural, human, and sincere. Someone had to make it happen, and it’s a relief KPDH did.

​@TheDutchGun "[Insert identity here] coded" just means the character is supposed to represent that identity although they technically aren't. For instance, the Na'vi in the Avatar franchise would be Native American coded. Knuckles the Echidna is black coded.

In theory it's a way to use allegory to analyze cultural differences, but often it's just an excuse for lazy leftie writers to (in the case of the various types of alphabet coded characters) promote deviant behaviour to children or (in the case of ethnically coded characters) work off their bigoted opinions on the various types of "brown people".

replies.author: @fluffsquirrel
replies.text:
@napoleonfeanor There is a point to this, I'll admit. However, unlike anime and whatever this film is, all of them strongly delineate between good and bad by alienating what is dark and evil.

They don't normalize dark elements in this way, they instead antagonize them to hopefully dissuade viewers from identifying with the dark unwanted concepts. Giving the clear and obvious bad guys an evil face and pit them against the hero makes the viewer feel invested in the hero and avoid considering the enemy's evil ways.

replies.author: @mmach8
replies.text:
@fluffsquirrel Of course, as a parent, you get to choose for your children.

Many "children's stories" contain dark elements which are conquered or may serve as object lessons: The Big Bad Wolf does NOT blow down the House of Bricks; the Lazy Grasshopper dies because it chose to play all summer without working to prepare for the coming winter. It's a matter of degree and choice for parents. It doesn't mean that said darker elements do not serve a purpose, depending upon context.

Regarding KDH, the "darker element" would be that what shames us does not have to be covered up, but can be confronted so that shame does not control us. There are also hints of redemption, that shame does not have to be what defines a person or dictates a negative course of their life.

Of course, a parent doesn't have to let their kids view it, but it still doesn't mean darker elements don't have a place in story-telling. I'm sure there are other stories available.

replies.author: @mmach8
replies.text:
@fluffsquirrel In the case of THIS particular story, Jinu's character was not exclusively evil in the sense that Gwi Ma and the other demons were inherently evil. Jinu made an terrible decision at the cost of his family and his soul.

Rumi hid and covered her shame of being half-demon (or whatever the marks represented). The other characters had their own elements of shame. It was only by confronting those things that they learned that shame did not have to dominate their character and lives.

Jinu saw Rumi do that and learned from it. She didn't "fix" him. He saw that redemption was possible and quit embracing the shame of his irreversible mistake. It didn't mean that what he did in the past was less wrong. It was still wrong. It meant that he could learn from it and move forward.

I and many others had to learn this lesson, sometimes several times. It's a good message.

Others may further discuss with their children or even apply that redemption is in Christ, rather than in a work done by self. (Totally an option, though it was not in the film.)

replies.author: @Hoodie_HD
replies.text:
⁠@fuentes196 Brienne of Tarth from the ASOIAF books is a good example of the kind of “tomboy” you’re describing. She is a good fighter and genuinely enjoys it, but she is also a romantic at heart who believes in the ideals of knighthood and wants to protect the weak and innocent. Hell she gets half her face chewed off by a psychotic cannibal while trying to protect a house full of orphaned children!

She also has a masculine style of dress and hair, but she doesn’t hate all men; she’s privately distrustful of most men because her potential suitors treated her terribly for her ugly, mannish appearance. She also treats feminine women with respect and courtesy even though she can’t relate to them very well, and vice versa.

That’s just the book version of the character though, in Game of Thrones her character was done badly.

author: @blindarcher1651
text:
A lot of solid symbolism, too. The use of color, for example: Everything about the Hunters is blue, from their logo to the Honmoon lines to their weapons ... except that when Rumi is doubting herself and her role, her sword takes on clearly visible pink tones, same as the Saja Boys' backdrops and Gwi-Ma himself. It's as if their influence is infecting her abilities, which is pretty much what's happening. In the climax, when she's finally come to terms with herself, it's back to blue.

Also, the "patterns" that mark her as part-demon started small, and grew as she pretended to be something she's not and concealed her patterned to hide the shame. It's notable that they spread to her voice during the very song, "Golden", in which the lyrics feature themes of "no lies" and "being who we're meant to be" -- themes she's NOT living in her own life. Her voice doesn't heal until she begins to accept herself.

It's a surprisingly deep movie, wrapped in a campy, silly animated feature.

Hello young man, this grumpy 62 year old man watched this last week, a enjoyed every minute.

I do watch a lot of Anime as I find the Japenes sense of humor very much like it used to be here up until the early 2000's. I also like that Anime does not treat the audiance as crettins, even most of those shows aimed at younger kids who are barely teenagers have an expectaion you are listen and paying attention to the story.

I'm not a K-Pop guy, but I am a big J-Rock fan, over the last decade I have not purchased any music from western bands or singers, that was not 25 or more years old. Yet I have purchased around 50 CDs form bands and singers from Japan. I don't speak/understand the language, I just like that quality of vocals and the expectation that everyone in a band is an excellent musician makes the music a joy to listen too.

Also unlike in the west these days where almost every singer does not sing, they drone like bagpipes (IMHO: the worst musical instrument ever created). I assume this is to make the sound engineers job auto-tuning the noise easier.

Listen to a CD/album I never get bored like I do listening to anything modern here, as you can find more msical variety in a single song than entire catalogues from the dull western so called tallent.

As an example you could check out (just enter the quoted text into YT's main search area):
 "Scandal A.M.D.K.J" and then compare it to "Scandal Mabataki", these are tracks 10 & 9 respectively from Scandal's 10th album called "Kiss from the Darkness".
 For me discovering this band back in 2016 was the point I restarted enjoying music again after more than a decade of apathy.

PS: One of my neighbors is a great lady in her 80's who is a massive fan of K-Pop and K-Dramas, people getting entertainment from around the world has become remarkably common.

author: @doublep1980
text:
The success of this movie isn't by chance.
One of the 2 directors worked previously on Coraline & Fantastic Mr.Fox. 
The animation team, are the same people who animated the Spiderverse movies.
The soundtrack is produced & performed by some top tier K-Pop artists. The main producer is Teddy Park,  the guy who produces all the Blackpink hits, EJAE ( singing voice of Rumi) is an acclaimed songwriter/ studio musician, same with Andrew Choi ( singing voice of Jinu) and they got 3 members of TWICE to sing the End Credit song (TWICE  are one of the biggest K-Pop girl groups, with a massive fandom specially in Asia.) 
They put some serious work into this movie.
The voice acting is also top notch, they even got Lee Byung-hun ( "I Saw the Devil" , the Frontman from Squid Game) to voice the demon king, he even did both the Korean & English dub.
P.S. I love how they incorporated traditional Korean folklore into this.
For example: the Tiger & Bird, are spirit animals from Korean mythology.

author: @FleetChadmiralDane
text:
What I really appreciate this movie is that it's 100% Korean. It didn't shy away from the fact that the countries in that part of the world are some of the most homogeneous societies on the planet, and the only reference we get to a different country is Zoey's background being born and raised in the United States, which is one sentence in the whole movie.
This is that kind of diversity in entertainment that Disney was delivering about 20 years ago with movies like Lilo & Stitch, Brother Bear, and Emperor's New Groove; they took a specific culture and made it look really fun and cool without the need to bad mouth other cultures.

author: @PrayerofPara
text:
Rumi's losing her voice is about her deception and struggle with the demon heritage - it's linked to shame and distrust in her relationships. And having someone accept her and care about her helps her accept herself and get over the demonic influence - hating her enemies and by extension herself, lies and deception, fighting fire with fire, etc. She accepts who she is and still works on her flaws and strives to be better. The negative influence that was crushing her soul and manifested in her losing her voice, i.e. the most precious part of her identity at the time is resolved as she realizes what the true problem is - not having a dark side, but the choices and compromises she makes to hide it, hate it, and hate others. Thus her voice comes back and is actually stronger than ever - a reflection of the state of her soul. And even then, it's not so simple and she has to go deeper with Ginu's betrayal and sacrifice.

Of course the main themes are not necessarily superficial or even external problems - that's why they work. The main themes are around shame and the struggle to do and be what you know is right when shame and guilt grip you; and trust, distrust, and self-protection. Nearly all the conflicts are around the compromises they make to protect themselves inwardly and it leads to real demonic behavior like deception, rejection, judgement, ignorance, and hatred culminating in them realizing that they've been acting just like the demons they try so hard to defeat. It's surprisingly deep, if very quickly summarized. The storytelling is actually in the music, perhaps even more than the plot. Listen to the big three songs in order - Golden, Free, What it Sounds Like - and not only the lyrics but how they portray what girls are thinking tells the story of their change and struggle for growth. It's actually really amazing how they could get just enough depth in storytelling in there within the fast and concise production. But it makes it far better to rewatch at the speed of kids and the frequency it plays over and over in my house 🙂

Yes, this. Celine raised Rumi, and taught her to feel disgust at this aspect of herself. Despite the fact that Rumi's patterns aren't based on any actual active sin she committed, but from how she was born. Meaning there was no guarantee of redemption. And it was entirely possible the golden honmoon could have rejected her.

Jinu is the first and only person who knew Rumi's secret and accepted her with that knowledge. Imagine going your whole life, thinking you are inherently repugnant, and having to hide part of yourself over which you have no actual control. No wonder it built up and took her voice. She was being silenced by her own shame, and the patterns were spreading because she was experiencing the compounding shame of her taught self loathing + her sense of betrayal toward and isolation from her friends.

author: @coffee_coder
text:
My daughter bugged me about this movie, so I renewed my lapsed subscription to get it. I was vaguely watching while doing housework but I got drawn in. It's a classic tale with a modern k-pop sugar pop glaze, and I thought, "Dang, I would have actually enjoyed going to the cinema with her to see this."

It’s a proper tale: women act like women, men act like men. It's a Romeo and Juliet story with humans and demons: love, trust, betrayal, and the idea that men can be beasts and women can tame them.


I wish we could have seen this in the cinema together. For once, I wouldn't have had to sit there feeling bored, embarrassed, ashamed, or repulsed during a kids' movie. Even though we've already seen it, I will take her to the cinema to see it if I get the chance.

That's it, Hollywood. Make proper tales that I can feel proud and confident to watch with my daughter, and you've bought my goodwill. Then by all means milk me dry for every bit of shiny, plastic tie merch that I will shell out for just to make my daughter smile.

author: @PristineTX
text: 5:59 The fun part about the themes presented in the movie is that they are even deeper than you think, the more you know about Korean culture/philosophy, especially for a movie that’s so silly and lighthearted when taken at surface level. A conventional Western audience may think the central theme is about struggling with identity…but they might equate it to modern western ideas like personal/sexual identity, and completely miss the fact that lurking below those more superficial concepts is a pretty well-handled, non-preachy critique of very KOREAN concepts. Cultural stuff like consciousness of family honor, filial piety, and the concept of Han (한) which forms uniquely-intense, uniquely-Korean notions of familial guilt that can carry across generations of family members. All Asian societies steeped in Confucianism carry a similar concept of generational familial responsibility/shame/resentment to various degrees, but Koreans definitely do.

replies.author: @jamesgollinger208
replies.text:
Can you explain the demons then, from a Korean perspective?  I did not understand them at all.

There are hordes of "born" demons who are just irredeemably evil.  Even when Zoey tries to talk to one it just smirks at her as though it's mocking her naivete for thinking it could be anything but a monster.  But there are also humans who become demons due to their actions, specifically making a deal (with evil incarnate) to give up responsibility for their own outcomes in exchange for also giving up their moral agency.  This puts "patterns" on them which they can only escape (as far as we know) by making an ultimate sacrifice for the greater good.

Then there's Zoey, who's born half-demon.  She has patterns seemingly without having done anything to earn them, but also they get worse when she tries to hide them.  When she admits to having them, they are still there but just aren't a problem anymore (as far as we know).

From a Western perspective, I'd say these patterns are a metaphor for behaviours that stem from the decision to subordinate morality to your personal desires.  They cause you to suffer constantly and control your actions, forcing you to do wrong not just to benefit yourself but for the sake of doing wrong.  But Zoey completely breaks that idea because she didn't really do anything to get the patterns in the first place but they get worse as she tries to hide them, the implication being that hiding them (or simply hating them) is a self-destructive act.  But in the end, once she comes clean, they aren't a problem at all.  So if patterns don't represent negative decisions, what are they and how do they connect to the demons?  Is this a cultural thing I'm not getting?

replies.author: @TheActualNorbert
replies.text: @jamesgollingee208 i think your reading a little too deep in this. I do see your confusion on the patterns having different meanings for different people. In general, the film is about the things we try to hide from our loved ones and how they only get worse until we let others in. For Rumi, they represent this idea in totality, whereas for Jinu they represent the main subsidiary of that theme, being the inherent guilt that grows as a result of these internal struggles. The patterns are the external consequences that result from keeping our pain hidden

author: @Im_B9ite
text: So I’ve been trying to get my wife to watch this movie like crazy for the last two months. She asked me why I’m so obsessed over this movie. I realized it it’s because it’s the first thing in years I’ve actually enjoyed and been excited about. I have spent and we have spent as a fan base so many years of bitching, complaining, and being disappointed in all of our favorite IP being destroyed that I’m just sick and tired of not having anything to enjoy anymore. All I’ve had left is the cathartic release of other fans bitching and complaining about woke ideology, destroying my childhood along with me, but that’s exhausting. This movie was like a lifeline to my inner child and gave me something to geek out about and enjoy. I can’t put my fandom into Star Wars, Marvel, video games, sci-fi novels, Lord of the rings were just about anything else I ever enjoyed. So now all of that energy is going into K-pop demon hunters because it’s the only thing I’ve had since endgame to be excited about.
